transcript: Hello. My name is Chris Chang, co-founder of Ghost, also the team behind Sandwiched.me, the Solana MEV Data Hub. Today, we will be demystifying the inner works of propAMMs. PropAMMs now represent 90% of aggregator swap volume. They are a hot topic on Solana. And today, we will pop the hood and discover how they work, and then show you how to reverse engineer them. And then we'll zoom in and look at the cat and mouse game between toxic flow and propAMMs. So what is propAMM? Let's review quickly. PropAMM looks like just another AMM from user's perspective. But they behave more like an on-chain market makers with active quoting logic. They're closed source with private strategies and curves. And operators actively manage inventory and quote. They don't take deposits and they often time don't have front end. So you usually interact with them through aggregators. Some aggregators lean on propAMM more than others, but they all make a big bet on them because their price is great. PropAMMs are very cool. They allow for permissionless swap. It's very competitive because they quote very tightly. And update a quote is very cheap, so they can react very quickly. Generally, we see about two to four propAMMs that dominates the industry. But landscape can shift very quickly. So we became very interested in propAMM about nine months ago. Since then, we improved our simulation and infra and built visualizer on Sandwiched.me. So let's dive into the tech. For a given propAMMs, we want to build a quote function that takes input, direction, and relevant accounts, and then go from there to get the output amounts as you would get if you swap on-chain. So we don't want to run SVM simulation to get the answer. The goal is to create a readable Rust port of swapping logic. So propAMMs don't provide decoded or open-source quote interface. To see the output at any given time, you kind of have to swap or do the simulation. But we don't want to do that, right? It doesn't reveal the how. So what we want to do is that, it's to educate you guys, it's fun, and closed and obfuscated sources can raise the bar, but they shouldn't be impossible to read. Here is some important gotcha. There isn't a universal quote. Different signers and transaction composition can actually leads to different outputs. More on this later. So before going over the tools and technique, let's go over one of the popular propAMMs and how they work. You probably already heard or maybe even used GoonFi. So let's look at how it works. Think about it as a oracle anchor market maker with some controls. So one is the inventory control, which keeps reserve at a target. And flow control, which rate limit how much can be traded at a given price tier per slot. Now the orange value up there are from the pool parameters. Stale oracle protection is explicit. If quote is too far away from the oracle functions, the quote simply refuses to quote. So on Layer 1, we have start from oracle. Layer 2, adjust price based on reserve and desired reserve. Layer 3, we penalize quote as oracle becomes stale. And Layer 4, we have execution across up to 10 lanes. And think about each lane as a separate tranche of liquidity of its own spread. The pool fill from the best tranche first and then slowly walk down the ladder. So 10 lanes per side. Each with its own cap, meters, and PPM spread. By the way, PPM is parts per million. There's also a built-in backfill logic where next slot after being consumed, refill half of the lane's capacity. And then the following slot after that, if it hasn't been consumed again, then refill completely. And you can see why quoting is not smooth. It's actually piecewise with explicit state per lane limit. And think about total out as a result of filling your order across 10 lanes, right? We scan from 0 to 9. And if the lane is full, then skip it. Otherwise, we take as much as we can and then fill it in. And for the slide, we compute an out_i, which using the lane's quote, and then add it to the total out. And then the final total out then is the sum of the slide-by-slide fills. So now let's dive into how to reverse engineer one of them. So three pillars that we have in mind. We have Simulator, Trace, and Static. A simulator is to execute transactions on the known state, right? And a trace is our fork of SBPF, which instructs the VM and logs structured trace of what happened. And statics, we decompile the on-chain program using Binary Ninja's High-Level Intermediate Language. And then lastly, we tie everything together using an AI Agent. So everything is based on an accurate simulator. You need to be able to answer what would this user get running the state and this input amount. Ghost SBPF is a fork where we instruct the VM and then so that every meaningful action such as function call, return, account read/write, operation, the output is a trace parquet file. From a trace, we can then rebuild the call summary. Here we see a summary of TesseraV swap, which you can see how, which function were invoked and how often. As well as how many times each function reads/write to the stack, the heap, and account data. It's a nice bird's eye view. With just the trace, we can then instantly follow the output backward and figure out how it was built up. And this is often a nice starting point because then you can see how obfuscated a program is, or isn't, and then get a sense of what's happening here. And now you can use the tool like Binary Ninja to decompile the propAMM, which you then need to install a plugin from OtterSec, dump the on-chain program, and then select Solana. TesseraV has 415 functions, but a swap only touches 68. We use our call summary to export those functions and then putting everything together into a repeatable prop pipeline. One script simulate a swap and then write a full report bundle. And then the idea is that for every single swap simulation, it becomes a case file for your AI Agent to consume, or you can read it. Topologically sorting code trees also means that agent can start from the grounds up, translate them first, and then keep on going up. The AI Agent will get to get all the traces, metadata, decompiled code. It's a goo layer. So now, let's talk about propAMM actually and the toxic flow. When we look at atomic arbs exclusively touch propAMM, they account for 5 to 10% of atomic arb revenue. And reacting to toxic flow is one of the top priority for propAMMs. And some propAMM actually give a different output depending on which aggregator program and method that invokes the swap. Many propAMM, the instruction, use instruction sysvar to inspect full transactions, who calls me, and what other swaps are present and ordering. If not called by whitelisted router, apply BPS penalty. TesseraV, missing a proper JitoDontFront, about 2 BPS penalty. If another propAMM swap is in the same transaction, HumidFi may add 25 BPS. And then SolFiV2, 3.6 BPS. This is evolving and bot adapt and cycle continues. So here's a arbitrage bot that has flow discrimination. They start from one transaction to multiple transaction now with a single bundle. And then we look into atomic arbs that has grown and now is the majority. And as you can see here, top pools last 30 seconds. And lastly, be on the lookout for unique dataset coming soon. The data will execute every on-chain transactions, swap a propAMM against other propAMMs, and then what the output would have been. And then kind of like ideal post-facto JIT. To understand the gap between execution and achievable execution. Thank you so much.

editorial.headline: PropAMMs Seize 90% of Solana Volume and Tax Toxic Flow
editorial.standfirst: Proprietary Automated Market Makers have quietly cornered the market, now executing the vast majority of aggregator swaps. Ghost co-founder Chris Chang exposes how these closed-source protocols programmatically discriminate against arbitrageurs to protect institutional margins.
key_takeaways: PropAMMs like HumidFi and TesseraV now control 90% of Solana's aggregator swap volume.
Protocols actively penalize 'toxic flow,' with HumidFi charging up to 25 basis points if rival pools appear in the same transaction.
Ghost is launching a unique dataset to visualize 'ideal post-facto JIT' execution gaps for institutional traders.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
The era of the passive liquidity pool is effectively over on Solana. Proprietary Automated Market Makers (PropAMMs) have silently risen to dominate the ecosystem, now processing **90% of all aggregator swap volume**. Unlike traditional AMMs that rely on static curves and public deposits, these protocols operate as closed-source, on-chain market makers with active quoting logic. Major players like **HumidFi** (often surpassing Raydium in volume), **SolFiV2**, and **TesseraV** (operated by market making giants like Wintermute) function more like high-frequency trading firms than decentralized exchanges. They maintain private strategies, manage their own inventory, and typically lack any public-facing frontend, relying entirely on aggregators for order flow.

To understand the mechanics behind this dominance, the team at **Ghost** (Sandwiched.me) reverse-engineered the bytecode of top pools. Using tools like Binary Ninja and custom SVM tracers, they deconstructed the logic of **GoonFi**, a popular PropAMM. Far from a simple `x*y=k` formula, GoonFi operates as an oracle-anchored market maker with sophisticated inventory and flow controls. Its liquidity is structured into **10 distinct execution lanes** (tranches), each with its own cap and spread. The protocol fills orders by walking down this ladder, applying dynamic rate limits per slot to prevent adverse selection—a level of complexity that mimics centralized exchange order books.

The most critical finding for institutional traders is the emergence of programmatic discrimination against "toxic flow." PropAMMs now inspect the `instruction sysvar` to analyze the full composition of a transaction before providing a quote. If the protocol detects it is being called by a non-whitelisted router or identifies a rival PropAMM in the same bundle, it applies aggressive price penalties. **HumidFi** creates a "PvP" environment by adding up to **25 basis points** to the spread if a competitor's swap is present in the transaction. Similarly, **SolFiV2** applies a **3.6 bps** penalty, and **TesseraV** adds roughly **2 bps**. This logic effectively taxes atomic arbitrage bots, which now account for 5-10% of revenue but face increasing resistance from these defensive algorithms.

As the landscape shifts toward this adversarial model, the gap between executed price and theoretical best price is widening for unsophisticated actors. Ghost is responding by releasing a dataset that simulates every on-chain transaction against rival PropAMMs to calculate an "ideal post-facto JIT" (Just-In-Time) execution. For VCs and developers, the message is clear: Solana's liquidity layer is no longer a public utility but a battleground of private, obfuscated strategies where execution quality depends heavily on who you are and how you route your trade.
